name: Marvin Harrison Jr.
blurb: Update: To no one’s surprise, the Arizona Cardinals took Marvin Harrison Jr. with the fourth overall pick in the draft. And his pre- to post-draft ZAP score is unchanged. Harrison brings an element to the Arizona offense that they simply don’t have. Trey McBride can operate the middle of the field and gobble up some easy looks, while Harrison will be winning on the outside, as he did at Ohio State. And, trust me: He’ll win. Prior to this draft and since 2011, we had only seen 14 wideouts get...

name: Malik Nabers
blurb: Update: The ZAP Model, as you pre-draft readers know, loves Malik Nabers. Not only is he slightly — slightly — ahead of Marvin Harrison Jr. in ZAP score, but he was tagged with a low-risk Draft Capital Delta. That means, despite his sixth overall draft capital, the model still sees him as an underrated player. That’s bonkers. The reason for that is about eight-fold. Naber’s 2023 saw him with a 3.82 receiving yards per team pass attempt, the highest max-season number from any Power Five wideou...

name: Rome Odunze
blurb: Update: Rome Odunze’s ZAP score is a little lower than the other wideouts who were picked in the top-10 because, quite simply, his age-adjusted collegiate production profile wasn’t as good. His top year yards per team pass attempt rate came in at 2.86, and that hit during his Senior season. Fortunately, even though he’s not an early-declare receiver, Odunze’s still got age on his side. He’s just a tad older than Marvin Harrison Jr., and that allowed his Breakout Score to sit at a respectable...

name: Xavier Worthy
blurb: Update: Xavier Worthy is small, and that frame gave him some issues at Texas. According to Matt Harmon’s Reception Perception, Worthy ranked below-average against press coverage, coming in well behind last year’s small wide receiver king, Tank Dell. The ZAP Model doesn’t factor in size, probably because it’s captured in draft capital. And Worthy got draft capital. He was selected 28th overall to Kansas City, making him just the fourth sub-170-pound wideout to get picked in the top-100 since 2...

name: Brian Thomas
blurb: Update: The Jacksonville Jaguars desperately needed some wide receiver help, so they went out and selected Brian Thomas with the 23rd overall pick in the draft. The ZAP Model loves it. Thomas’ Draft Capital Delta profile pegs him as a “low risk” bet, but there’s definitely more iffiness to his profile than the model is seeing. Thomas was below a 1.0 yards per team pass attempt rate across his first two seasons of college ball before breaking out as a Junior. That breakout was pretty massive —...

name: Brock Bowers
blurb: Elite prospect; Weird spot with Mayer in the mix

name: Jonathon Brooks
blurb: Update: There was a chance that we weren’t going to see a running back hit a ZAP score of 90 this year for the first time in its history, and then the rebuilding Carolina Panthers took Jonathon Brooks with the 46th pick. That shot him up to 93.8. Now we’re cookin’. Brooks is the best running back in this class. He tore his ACL in November, ending his season early. He finished 2023 with an impressive 1.99 total yards per team play rate (prorated, of course), and his reception share was 11.0%....

name: Caleb Williams
source_quote: 8   Caleb Williams       QB      1     5     8   Xavier Worthy        WR      4     4

name: Jayden Daniels
source_quote: 9   Jayden Daniels       QB      2     5     9   Brian Thomas         WR      5     4

name: Ricky Pearsall
blurb: Update: When Ricky Pearsall’s write-up was published back in early March, he was projected to maybe be a second-round pick. Maybe. Back then, it was easy to call him a rookie draft target. Now, he’s going to rise up boards thanks to his relatively surprising Round 1 draft capital. That presents more of a problem. History doesn’t favor Pearsall. Specifically, his Breakout Score of 57.7 is a pretty big concern. Since 2011, there hasn’t been a single first-round wide receiver hit who had a Break...

name: Ladd McConkey
blurb: Update: When it comes to the production metrics that go into the ZAP Model, Ladd McConkey’s not looking so hot. His top year in receiving yards per team pass attempt is only 1.63, the third-lowest mark in the class. After getting selected in the second round by the Chargers, McConkey joins three other Round 2 wideouts since 2011 who had a best-season yards per team pass attempt rate under 1.70. Those three guys? Aaron Dobson, Mecole Hardman, and Van Jefferson. There’s a key difference between...

name: Keon Coleman
blurb: Update: Buffalo was probably the best landing spot for a wide receiver in this year’s draft, and Keon Coleman was the Bills’ selection at the top of the second round. That, naturally, will raise his rookie draft cost. The ZAP Model doesn’t see Coleman as an unbelievable prospect. He couldn’t get to a max-season yards per team pass attempt of 2.0 in college, something 74.4% of the class accomplished. That led to a Breakout Score that was barely above average at 55.5. We’ve had some hits in the...

name: Trey Benson
blurb: Update: The size-speed combination is there for Trey Benson. He ran a 4.39 40-yard dash at the NFL Combine this year at 216 pounds, giving him the second-best Speed Score in the class. The ZAP Model is into that, but it’s not into everything about his profile. Benson got off to a slow start in college at Oregon after tearing his ACL as a Freshman. He redshirted and then had only six carries during his second season as a Duck. That led to a transfer to Florida State in 2022, where he led the b...

name: Drake Maye
source_quote: 14   Drake Maye           QB      3     7    14   Ricky Pearsall       WR      6     5

name: JJ McCarthy
source_quote: 15   JJ McCarthy          QB      4     7    15   Ladd McConkey        WR      7     5

name: Ja'Lynn Polk
blurb: Update: Thank goodness for Teammate Score, because it’s making Ja’Lynn Polk’s ZAP Model profile look much more relevant. As many of you know, he played alongside Rome Odunze and Jalen McMillan in college, and that naturally impacted his overall numbers. But his overall numbers still weren’t fantastic. Polk’s Breakout Score — again, we’re all on the same page here that teammate context is important — is just 38.5. He’s now the seventh wide receiver in the ZAP Model database to go in Round 2 wi...

name: Xavier Legette
blurb: Update: Xavier Legette has the strangest production profile you’ll find from a wide receiver prospect. We got mostly nothing from him during his first four years at South Carolina, with his single-season receiving yards total topping out at 167. And then the 2023 season happened. Legette went from a yards per team pass attempt of just 0.59 in 2022 to a rate of 3.05 last year. He’s one of just eight wideouts in the class with a best-season number north of 3.0. Since that figure was so strong,...

name: Adonai Mitchell
blurb: Update: Just a couple of years ago, the Colts took an athletic wide receiver who had poor collegiate production with the 53rd overall pick in the draft. Here in 2024, Indy selected an athletic wide receiver who had poor collegiate production with the 52nd overall pick in the draft. It appears they have a type. Adonai Mitchell’s top comparable in the ZAP Model is Alec Pierce. That’s who Indianapolis selected two drafts ago. And, as we know, Pierce hasn’t exactly worked out. The ZAP Model does...

name: Jermaine Burton
blurb: Update: There’ve been some off-the-field questions surrounding Jermaine Burton, and that could be the reason he was drafted in Round 3 this year instead of Round 2. But he got decent draft capital when he landed with the Bengals at 80th overall. And he got Joe Burrow, too. Burton’s been a relatively underrated player throughout the draft process. He started off at Georgia, and, in 2021, he was second on the team in receiving yards. As a Sophomore. On a team that had Brock Bowers, Ladd McConke...

name: MarShawn Lloyd
blurb: Update: There are plenty of MarShawn Lloyd truthers out there, so it’s a little scary that the ZAP Model is labeling his profile as a “high risk” one. The reason for that is because he’s the seventh-oldest back in this year’s class — he’s a good bit over 23 — his Breakout Score is 31.2, and he never got to a 10% best- season reception share. Looking back to 2011, Lloyd appears to be the only 23-year-old running back in the ZAP Model who received top-100 draft capital after failing to reach a...

name: Jaylen Wright
blurb: Update: Of course. Of course Jaylen Wright, who ran a sub-4.4 40-yard dash and had the highest explosive play rate in this year’s running back class…of course he lands with Mike McDaniel in Miami. Of course. The fit is clearly there. The Dolphins zone scheme allowed speedster Raheem Mostert to lead the NFL in touchdowns last year, and De’Von Achane is coming off the most efficient per-rush season of any moderately high-volume back in NFL history. Jaylen Wright will do just fine when he gets a...

name: Troy Franklin
blurb: Update: Troy Franklin’s projected draft capital in the pre-draft version of this guide was in the early second round. He ended up getting selected in the early fourth round. The reason for the slip is probably related to his size. Franklin’s under 180 pounds, and according to Matt Harmon’s Reception Perception, he struggled against press coverage. That’s not going to get any easier in the NFL, and it could lead him to going down the path as some of his top comparables in the ZAP Model. Jalin...

name: Roman Wilson
blurb: Update: With Arthur Smith in town, the Steelers are going to lean more on the run. The Falcons were bottom-five in the league in pass rate during two of his three seasons there as head coach. As offensive coordinator in Tennessee during the two years prior, the Titans were 29th and 30th in pass rate. Pittsburgh beefing up their offensive line in the draft isn’t a surprise, but, even with this run-heavy offense, they also had a huge void at wide receiver that needed to be filled after they tra...

name: Bo Nix
source_quote: 24   Bo Nix               QB      5     9    24   Troy Franklin        WR     13     7

name: Ben Sinnott
blurb: Decent destination; Would've liked slightly better production and athleticism
source_quote: Ben Sinnott           Kansas State       Commanders   83.9   slightly better production and

name: Malachi Corley
blurb: Update: Since 2011, the ZAP Model has seen 11 wide receivers get selected in the top-100 of the NFL Draft after failing to register a Breakout Score. Among that group, only Terry McLaurin was able to do anything of substance in fantasy football during his first three years in the league. Malachi Corley has a Breakout Score of zero. Corley’s top season in yards per team pass attempt ended up being only 2.08, a number that’s not all that impressive considering the competition he faced at Wester...

name: Javon Baker
blurb: Update: That Patriots are getting weapons for Drake Maye. And unlike the last regime, they selected a couple of solid prospects in the first four rounds of the draft. Ja’Lynn Polk was their early-second-round selection, and Javon Baker was their fourth-round one. Baker started his collegiate journey at Alabama, where he caught just nine passes in two seasons. He then transferred to Central Florida where he immediately produced as a Junior, catching 56 balls for almost 800 yards. That resulted...

name: Blake Corum
blurb: Update: Well, that’s not ideal, is it? Blake Corum was drafted by the Rams, which is kind of funny, because former Ram Darrell Henderson is one of his top comps. What’s not funny is the fantasy football mess that this brings. With Kyren Williams coming off the season he had, Corum’s unlikely to grab a big share of the team’s backfield. The ZAP Model isn’t in love with him anyway. He’s a little undersized, had a totally ordinary Speed Score, and just had solid-but-not-great marks in reception...

name: Kimani Vidal
blurb: Update: Just two running backs in the ZAP Model database measured under 5’9’’ at the NFL Combine while hitting an above-average Breakout Score and a 105 Speed Score. One of them is D’Andre Swift. The other is Troy’s Kimani Vidal. Vidal had the eighth-highest offensive grade among running backs across college football last year according to PFF, and he has above-average marks versus this year’s class in Breakout Score, forced missed tackles per attempt, and receiving yards per route run. Of co...

name: Malik Washington
blurb: Update: The best Day 3 wide receiver pairing might be Malik Washington going to Miami. Yeah, yeah, I know — the Dolphins have two target hogs on the roster already in Tyreek Hill and Jaylen Waddle. What they don’t have is a reliable slot receiver, and that’s exactly what Washington is. After a tough time producing at Northwestern for four years, Washington transferred to Virginia for one final season of college ball. There, he caught 110 passes for 1,426 yards and 9 scores, skyrocketing his b...

name: Jalen McMillan
blurb: Update: The ZAP Model is into Jalen McMillan. Sure, his 74.6 score isn’t anything to write home about, but, compared to draft capital, the model’s looking at him favorably. McMillan landed with Tampa Bay in the third round. He ran roughly 90% of his routes from the slot in 2022 and 2023 — his only productive seasons at Washington — so we should probably expect that kind of role with the Buccaneers. Trey Palmer occupied that area of the field on 57% of his routes last season, which led the tri...

name: Michael Penix
source_quote: 32   Michael Penix        QB      6    10    32   Spencer Rattler      QB      7     9

name: Ray Davis
blurb: Update: Ray Davis has a pretty complete production profile. His top season in reception share is an impressive 15.6%, a number matched by just one other player in this year’s class. His best season in total yards per team play is north of 2.0, too. Only five backs in the class got to that level. Part of the reason Davis was able to get to those numbers was because he played five years of college ball. He kicked things off at Temple and played two years there. Then he went to Vandy for two sea...

name: Jacob Cowing
blurb: Update: Jacob Cowing is a Day 3 favorite of the ZAP Model. His Draft Capital Delta is neutral, but his Breakout Score of 74.3 is 12th-best in the class after he tallied some monster seasons at UTEP. He played three years there — two of them had elite receiving yards per team pass attempt rates — before transferring to Arizona. His production was still OK at Arizona, where he hit prorated yards per team pass attempt rates of 1.82 and 2.25 during his two campaigns there. Size is a concern for C...

name: Ainias Smith
blurb: Update: Ainias Smith’s game is easy to like. He played running back as a Sophomore at Texas A&M (or was listed as one), where he ran the ball 40 times and caught 43 passes. He then was a more traditional wide receiver as a Junior, and, as a Senior, he hurt his leg and missed the majority of the season. He went back for another year in 2023, where he put together the best receiving season of his career. There’s so much versatility with Smith, so he’s just easy to fall in love with. And that’s...

name: Tyrone Tracy
blurb: Update: When the pre-draft version of this guide was released in early March, there was very little chatter around Purdue’s Tyrone Tracy. He started picking up some social media steam over the last month or so, and it all culminated with him getting selected in the fifth round by the New York Giants. There’s not much happening on that Giants running back depth chart. They signed Devin Singletary this offseason, and while Singletary’s had success under Brian Daboll in the past, that’s not a lo...

name: Ja'Tavion Sanders
blurb: Pretty good production; Pretty good landing spot; Above-average prospect

name: Rasheen Ali
blurb: Update: We didn’t see much production from Rasheen Ali when he was a Freshman at Marshall, but that changed dramatically as a Sophomore in 2021. He rushed for over 1,400 yards during his second year, catching 45 passes in the process. His 13.9% reception share that season ended up being a career high, and it also ended up being sixth-best in this year’s class. Meanwhile, his 68.9 Breakout Score was eighth-best. Outside of the model, Ali’s forced missed tackles per attempt rate in his top seas...

name: Devontez Walker
blurb: Update: Devontez Walker going to Baltimore in Round 4 is a great real football fit, but it may not turn into a great fantasy football one. Walker began playing college football at North Carolina Central in 2020, but he actually didn’t get run because the season was cancelled due to COVID. He then transferred to Kent State in 2021, where caught just 5 passes for 124 yards and a score. We didn’t get sincere production from Walker until 2022. He finished that season with a yards per team pass at...

name: Theo Johnson
blurb: Elite athlete; poor production profile; Interesting landing spot

name: Luke McCaffrey
blurb: Update: Luke McCaffrey — who, yes, is Christian’s brother — started out his college career at Nebraska in 2019. As a quarterback. He was a mobile passer who didn’t get a ton of playing time, and he eventually ended up at Rice, where he gave quarterback one last try. In 2022, he officially moved to wide receiver. McCaffrey’s lower production totals aren’t surprising when you consider his journey. He only played wide receiver for two seasons, and it’s kind of impressive that he still posted yar...

name: Dylan Laube
blurb: Update: As a reminder, there’s only one running back in the ZAP Model’s history who was able to hit a best-season reception share above 25%. And it’s Las Vegas Raider Dylan Laube. Laube is at an analytical advantage in that he played for an FCS school. Being the running back for New Hampshire isn’t the same as being the running back for Alabama. We have to keep that in mind when we’re looking at his production metrics. With that being said, his playing for a smaller program definitely hurt hi...

name: Erick All
blurb: Arguably second-best production profile behind Bowers; Strong landing spot

name: Braelon Allen
blurb: Update: The ZAP Model likes Braelon Allen, but after a draft process where he didn’t run the 40, the algorithm cooled on him a bit. There’s nothing landing spot-related in the ZAP Model, but if there was, Allen’s score would be even lower. The Jets decided to take him at the end of the fourth round, derailing any sort of early-career production. He’s now backing up one of the best running backs in football in Breece Hall. Allen could step in and perform with an injury, though. He’s had a wild...

name: Isaac Guerendo
blurb: Update: Isaac Guerendo weighed in at 221 pounds at the combine, making him the fourth-heaviest back in this year’s class. He then went out and ran a 4.33 40, giving him the best Speed Score — by far — in the group. It was actually the third-best Speed Score the ZAP Model database has seen from a back. How much does that really matter? Well, Guerendo’s production profile was pretty mediocre. He failed to register a Breakout Score after playing four years at Wisconsin and one at Louisville. He...

name: Jaheim Bell
blurb: Model loves him; Small size for a productive tight end

name: Audric Estime
blurb: Update: We saw the right kind of year-over-year progression with Audric Estime’s production at Notre Dame. As a Freshman, he only carried the ball seven times and had a 0.17 prorated total yards per team play rate. That jumped up to 1.21 as a Sophomore. He then finished off his collegiate career with a 1.99 tally in 2023. Estime is the second-youngest running back in this year’s class, and that helped him out in the ZAP Model. His Breakout Score of 79.3 is a top-five one among backs who were...

name: Will Shipley
blurb: Update: We’re probably not going to see much Will Shipley across his first two seasons in the NFL. The Eagles selected him in the fourth round, and, as we all know, Philly signed Saquon Barkley to a lucrative contract over the offseason. Their first potential out with said contract occurs in 2026. As a player, Shipley is a do-it-all back. He just doesn’t “do it all” exceptionally well. Shipley’s Breakout Score of 60.5 is OK, and it’s thanks to some decent production at Clemson as a Freshman....

name: Bucky Irving
blurb: Update: Things haven’t been great for Bucky Irving ever since the NFL Combine. We knew he was on the smaller side, and then he confirmed it by weighing in at 192 pounds. He then went out and ran a 4.55 40, giving him a sub-90 Speed Score. And that was on top of a production profile that, to the ZAP Model, wasn’t great. His Breakout Score of 49.8 is a below-average one. The landing spot in Tampa Bay is…interesting? If Irving gets run in the NFL, it’s going to be as a receiving back first and f...

name: Cody Schrader
blurb: Cody Schrader got his college football start at Truman State University, a Division II program in *Googles* Missouri. He had one monster year there before becoming a Mizzou walk-on in 2022. Schrader’s a unique prospect for the model because his numbers are a little inflated after playing D2 ball. Without accounting for his huge 2021 season at Truman State, he’s a fairly ordinary prospect. When you consider it, he looks a good bit better, with his Breakout Score going from 19 to about 65. The...

name: Daijun Edwards
blurb: On paper, Daijun Edwards’ profile isn’t very good. He never reached an 8% reception share, giving him the third-lowest mark in the class. He’s also a little undersized and failed to break out until late into his collegiate career. From 2011 to 2021, we had 67 running backs get drafted after Pick 100 who weighed in under 210 pounds. Just 13 of those 67 had sub-8% receptions shares. And just 1 of those 13 smaller, low-reception-share backs had a max season across their first three in the league...

name: Dillon Johnson
blurb: Dillon Johnson started his collegiate journey at Mississippi State, where he played three years under the late Mike Leach. There, he wasn’t used much as a runner — he failed to reach 500 rushing yards in each season — but, as we’ve seen before with Leach running backs, Johnson was deployed often as a pass-catcher. He hauled in reception shares of 9.7%, 12.6%, and 13.3% over his three seasons as a Bulldog. Johnson transferred to Washington for his final season, and that reception share dropped...

name: Emani Bailey
blurb: At face value, Emani Bailey looks like a very obvious player to avoid. He’s undersized. His top year in reception share is well below 10%. He didn’t have a special Breakout Score. His Speed Score was just below 90. None of the ZAP Model inputs look especially good for Bailey. Bailey’s comparables, though, make you wonder. One of them is Devonta Freeman. Like Bailey, Freeman was an undersized runner with mediocre production who ran a pretty slow 40 before he got to the league. And, in the leag...

name: Frank Gore Jr.
blurb: We watched Frank Gore destroy defenses for years, and now it’s his son’s turn. Man, I feel old. Gore doesn’t have the same frame as his dad, but he still has pretty good tackle- breaking ability. He forced missed tackles in his best season at a 36.6% rate, per PFF, which was a top-10 number in the class. There’s a chance we see Gore deployed as a pass-catcher in the NFL, which would be good news for his fantasy value. His top-season in reception share was a healthy 14.6%, and he hit a solid 1...

name: George Holani
blurb: For a player who may go undrafted, George Holani has a pretty strong production profile. His 56.4 Breakout Score is above average within the class, and his 13.8% reception share is eighth-best. He also had above-average marks in total yards per team play, missed tackles forced per attempt, and yards per route run. There just may not be anything special about Holani, which is the problem. He’s certainly not an explosive back, having generated a 10-plus yard run on just 13.5% of his totes durin...

name: Isaiah Davis
blurb: Update: It’s almost like the Jets were using the ZAP Model to draft this year. After taking Braelon Allen on Day 3, New York followed it up and, for whatever reason, took Isaiah Davis in Round 5. Davis could potentially beat out Allen for the RB2 spot on the team, but there’s no chance either guy is jumping over Breece Hall. Davis is an underrated prospect. He played four seasons at South Dakota State, with one of those campaigns being a COVID-stricken one. He finished his collegiate career w...

name: Jaden Shirden
blurb: The ZAP Model database says that there’ve been 22 running backs who weighed in under 190 pounds at the NFL Combine since 2011. Of those 22, only Tarik Cohen, De’Von Achane, and Keaton Mitchell have given fantasy managers any sort of material output. Those aren’t great odds, but get this: Cohen, Achane, and Mitchell had Breakout Scores that ranked in the top-six within that group of 22 backs. Jaden Shirden’s 87.5 Breakout Score puts him right there with those guys. Counting on a sub-190-pound...

name: Jase McClellan
blurb: Yes, we’ve seen plenty of W’s at running back out of Alabama in recent history. Jahmyr Gibbs is a monster. Najee Harris has been serviceable. Brian Robinson had a great 2023 season. Josh Jacobs. Damien Harris. Each of the last five running backs to go to the NFL Combine from Bama have been good to elite. Is Jase McClellan really going to make it six in a row? Well, he’s got the right size for the next level. Or, at least, size that typically comes with a big workload. He’s still not 22 years...

name: Jawhar Jordan
blurb: It’s really hard to buy into Jawhar Jordan as a fantasy football contributor. Jordan’s the fourth-smallest back in this class by weight, and he’s the tiniest by BMI. You can reference De’Von Achane all day, but Achane had an elite production profile. Jordan does not. Jordan’s Breakout Score of 18.8 ranks in the bottom-six among backs who went to the combine this year. His best-season reception share is at just 8.5%. His best- season in explosive run rate, per Pro Football Focus data, was belo...

name: Keilan Robinson
blurb: Update: The best part about Keilan Robinson’s ZAP Model profile is that he played with Bijan Robinson, Roschon Johnson, and Jonathon Brooks. That’s his excuse for having a Breakout Score of zero. From a running back perspective, Keilan Robinson isn’t likely to be asked to do much in the NFL. At 5’8’’, 191 pounds, he’s a change-of-pace guy who could help a team out — the Jaguars — dramatically on special teams. Robinson’s receiving numbers aren’t too shabby, though, especially when you conside...

name: Kendall Milton
blurb: Kendall Milton was never a workhorse back at Georgia. To reiterate some of the things that were said about his teammate, Daijuan Edwards, yes, Georgia does usually split up their backfields. And, yes, Milton played alongside some pretty legit competition in college. But at least Edwards had some semblance of a production profile. Milton’s the bigger of the two backs who shared the Georgia backfield this past year, coming in at 220 pounds. Even with that size, though, Milton forced missed tack...

name: Michael Wiley
blurb: Michael Wiley has the pass-catching profile we look for in a fantasy back. His top season in reception share was nearly 14% (top-10 in the class), and he’s one of two running backs in this year’s draft group to have hit a best-season yards per route run greater than 2.0. Unfortunately, Wiley’s Breakout Score was lacking after never having a ton of success on the ground at Arizona. He saw more than 100 carries in just one of five seasons, maxing out his single-season rushing yards at 771. His...

name: Miyan Williams
blurb: Miyan Williams backed up TreVeyon Henderson at Ohio State, leading to a poorer production profile. He’s one of five backs in this year’s class to have failed to register a Breakout Score. Among the players in that group, he was second-worst in reception share. Receiving probably won’t be at the forefront for Williams at the next level, but he may become a reasonable early-down rusher. He’s got a ridiculously thick frame, standing at just 5’9’’ and weighing in at 229 pounds at the combine. His...

name: AJ Barner
blurb: Average athleticism with below-averge production

name: Cade Stover
blurb: Reunited with CJ Stroud; Relatively ordinary profile

name: Devin Culp
blurb: Great athlete; Poor draft capital; Not young

name: Jared Wiley
blurb: Below-average production with decent athleticism; Older

name: Tanner McLachlan
blurb: Oldest tight end drafted; Good size and decent speed

name: Tip Reiman
blurb: Epic athletic profile; Horrific landing spot

name: Anthony Gould
blurb: Update: The Colts took Anthony Gould in Round 5, and, not to be rude, but you can essentially ignore the pick for fantasy purposes. OK, OK. We should be open to anything being possible. And Gould’s a little interesting, I suppose, because, at his smaller size, he actually played a lot on the perimeter in college. Per Pro Football Focus, he ran more than 200 routes in just one of his four collegiate seasons, which came last year. And, last season, he had a slot rate of just 7.4%. The year prio...

name: Brenden Rice
blurb: When it comes to the things the ZAP Model looks at, Brenden Rice doesn’t grade out all that well. He never got anything going when he started his college career at Colorado, and while his production increased at Southern Cal in 2022 and 2023, his numbers still weren’t special. His best-season in yards per team pass attempt was only 1.86, and his other relevant season was at 1.19. That all led to a Breakout Score of zero. Since 2011, of the wide receivers selected after the first round and thr...

name: Bub Means
blurb: Update: Bub Means got to college in 2019, playing defensive back for Tennessee. He then transferred to Louisiana Tech and sat out 2020 before playing a season of wide receiver for them in 2021. After that, Means transferred to Pitt (#Hail2Pitt), where he’d play his final two collegiate seasons, tallying a best- season yards per team pass attempt rate of just 2.00. Means has good size, and he can definitely get down the field. His average depth of target last season was 17.7. He also ranked fi...

name: Cornelius Johnson
blurb: Cornelius Johnson was at Michigan for five seasons, and while he wasn’t terrible, he wasn’t elite, either. His best year in yards per team pass attempt was 1.67 (he had a higher prorated one during the COVID year, but he only played four games), and his top season in yards per route run ranks fifth-worst in the class. On the positive side, Johnson did finish second on Michigan this past season in receptions, trailing Roman Wilson — a favorite of many in this class — by one. He has great size...

name: Devaughn Vele
blurb: The ZAP Model database includes over 660 wide receivers. The average wide receiver age on draft day, since 2011, has been 22.4. Meaning the ordinary wideout has been just shy of 22-and-a-half years old prior to his particular NFL Draft. No wide receiver in the database has come out of college football older than Devaughn Vele. My man is going to be almost 26-and-a-half years old when this year’s draft starts. That’s a year-and-a-half older than Justin Jefferson. As in, Justin Jefferson today,...

name: Isaiah Williams
blurb: There are plenty of smaller, late-round slot players in this class. Isaiah Williams is one of the most intriguing. Williams kicked off his collegiate career at Illinois as a quarterback. He got some playing time and had 63 pass attempts in a shortened 2020 COVID season, but he eventually switched to wide receiver in 2021. So, really, he hasn’t been at this very long. Williams immediately produced. He tallied 525 receiving yards and a 1.63 receiving yards per team pass attempt rate in 2021. In...

name: Jalen Coker
blurb: Jalen Coker is the first Holy Cross wide receiver in the ZAP Model’s database. Needless to say, the Crusaders don’t have a standout program. Coker’s still a pretty interesting prospect. His numbers are naturally inflated because he went to a small school, but it’s not like every small-school wideout is able to get to the 4.11 yards per team pass attempt level that Coker got to as a Senior. He’s just the 22nd player since 2011 to have a best-season yards per team pass attempt rate above 4.0. B...

name: Jamari Thrash
blurb: Update: Jamari Thrash has the third-best max-season receiving yards per team pass attempt rate in this year’s class. The reason he’s got a “high risk” Draft Capital Delta is because it took him a while to get to that number. He was at Georgia State for four years, and he needed all four seasons to just get over 500 receiving yards in a single campaign. That was the year where he dominated and had a 3.77 yards per team pass attempt, over two yards higher than his number from the previous seaso...

name: Jha'Quan Jackson
blurb: Update: Jha’Quan Jackson has a smaller frame, and he played the majority of his snaps out of the slot during his five years at Tulane. We should expect him to be limited to that kind of role at the start of his NFL career. Considering the team that drafted him — the Titans — that’s totally fine. They’ll have veteran wideouts DeAndre Hopkins and Calvin Ridley getting a lot of reps on the perimeter, while Treylon Burks, Kyle Philips, and, now, Jha’Quan Jackson battle for slot work. Jackson’s nu...

name: Johnny Wilson
blurb: Update: Very-large-human-being Johnny Wilson ended up going later in the draft than most expected. The Eagles snagged him in Round 6, and they got a player who could, hypothetically, back up their studly perimeter wideouts in the offense. Wilson’s production marks weren’t all that bad in college. After failing to post much production at Arizona State, he transferred to Florida State in 2022. There, he immediately put up a 2.27 yards per team pass attempt rate, giving us a Breakout Score of 37...

name: Jordan Whittington
blurb: Another wideout who competed with NFL-quality talent in college is Jordan Whittington. You’ve already seen the scores for Whittington’s teammates Adonai Mitchell and Xavier Worthy, and there’s no reason to necessarily prefer Whittington over those two players. He does have a couple of noteworthy items to his scouting report, though. Whittington was actually listed as a running back during a Freshman season that was cut short after one game due to injury. He was officially a wide receiver star...

name: Lideatrick Griffin
blurb: Lideatrick Griffin is on the smaller side for a wide receiver, so his 90.6% slot rate in 2023 isn’t very surprising. What is kind of surprising is that he barely played in the slot at all during his three other seasons at Mississippi State. In 2022, for example, PFF has Griffin with a 2.0% slot rate. Griffin’s numbers were best — by far — during his 2023 campaign when he manned the slot. His season high in yards per team pass attempt from 2020 to 2022 was just 0.79. Last year? 1.89. That’s st...

name: Marcus Rosemy-Jacksaint
blurb: Georgia has one of the most competitive programs in the country, but that doesn’t totally excuse Marcus Rosemy-Jacksaint’s production profile. Or lack thereof. Rosemy-Jacksaint does well in the model’s hyphenated last name metric — he matches players like JuJu Smith—Schuster and Marquez Valdes-Scantling — but the rest of his numbers are really lacking. He failed to register a Breakout Score after hitting a max-season yards per team pass attempt of just 1.26. We’ve only seen two wide receivers...

name: Ryan Flournoy
blurb: Ryan Flournoy’s another small-program wideout who could get drafted on Day 3, but his numbers aren’t as strong as someone like, say, Jalen Coker. Aside from not playing at a Power Five school, one of the biggest red flags to Flournoy’s statistical profile is his age. He’ll be 24-and-a-half years old during this year’s draft, making him the second-oldest wide receiver in the class. Since 2011, the best wide receiver in the ZAP Model database who was that old coming out is probably Byron Pringl...

name: Tahj Washington
blurb: Tahj Washington, compared to the players ranked this low in this year’s class, does have some more favorable marks in his profile. His best season in yards per route run is 10th-best in the class, and it was better than USC teammate Brenden Rice. Washington actually averaged more yards and receptions per game than Rice in each of the last two seasons. The difference is that Washington doesn’t have the standard NFL wide receiver build, and he ran 88% of his routes out of the slot during this t...

name: Tayvion Robinson
blurb: Tayvion Robinson, like so many NFL-hopeful wide receivers these days, transferred during his time at college. He started things off at Virginia Tech where he played for three seasons, and his final two came at the University of Kentucky. All in all, for a player expected to go undrafted — or, at least, that’s what it’s looking like now — Robinson’s numbers aren’t that bad. He never had a crazy high yards per team pass attempt rate, but his Breakout Score is 51.6 after getting worthwhile produ...

name: Xavier Weaver
blurb: For someone with such a poor draft capital projection, Xavier Weaver’s profile isn’t all that bad. His Breakout Score is 59.6 and his Fantasy Points Score is 46.9. Only 13 wide receivers in the 2024 class were able to get to those levels. Weaver played at South Florida for four years before transferring to Coach Prime’s Colorado Buffaloes in 2023. He gave us yards per team pass attempt rates above 2.0 in each of his final three seasons, including a 2022 campaign with USF that saw him reach a...
